experiment_waves.name: Stop the Bleeding
experiment_waves.theme: Cut ad waste and add free keywords
experiment_waves.why_together: These are all PPC/backend changes that don't affect the listing content shoppers see. No overlap, so results won't contaminate each other.
experiment_waves.wave_success_criteria: Total daily ad spend drops to $15 or less. No revenue decrease from organic sales.

experiment_waves.name: Title Optimization
experiment_waves.why_together: Title is independent from PPC changes in Wave 1. Both can run at the same time because they affect different parts of the funnel (title affects CTR from search, PPC affects which searches you appear in).
experiment_waves.wave_success_criteria: CTR from search results improves, sessions increase, or CVR holds steady/improves within 14 days.

experiment_waves.name: Bullet Rewrite
experiment_waves.why_together: EXP-031 and EXP-033 are included inside the EXP-022 full bullet rewrite. They are one single change, not three separate ones.
experiment_waves.why_wait: Need to measure the title change impact first. If you change title AND bullets at the same time, you won't know which one moved the needle.
experiment_waves.wave_success_criteria: CVR increases from ~3.38% to 4.5%+ within 14 days.

experiment_waves.name: PPC Relaunch + Image
experiment_waves.why_together: EXP-027 and EXP-030 are both new exact-match PPC campaigns targeting different keywords, so they won't compete. EXP-021 (image overlay) affects visual appeal in search results, which is independent from PPC keyword targeting.
experiment_waves.why_wait: Your listing needs to be optimized (title + bullets) BEFORE you start spending money on ads again. Sending paid traffic to an unoptimized listing wastes money.
experiment_waves.wave_success_criteria: At least 1 ad-attributed sale within 14 days. ACoS under 50%.

experiment_waves.name: A+ Content Upgrade
experiment_waves.why_together: Both are A+ content changes (headline and durability section). They appear in different parts of the A+ module and should be published together as one update.
experiment_waves.why_wait: A+ content affects shoppers who scroll past the bullets. You want to fix the top of the listing first (title, bullets, images) before optimizing the bottom.
experiment_waves.wave_success_criteria: CVR increases from post-Wave-3 baseline. Time on page increases.

experiment_waves.name: Structural Cleanup
experiment_waves.why_together: These are operational tasks (inventory removal, review requests, bundle deactivation, CVR audit) that don't affect listing content. They can be done anytime without interfering with A/B testing.
experiment_waves.wave_success_criteria: Storage fees reduced. Review velocity increases. Bundle deactivated.

active_experiments.element: PPC - Negative keyword purge on zero-converting targets
active_experiments.hypothesis: Negating/pausing targets with clicks but zero sales in 14 days will reduce wasted ad spend by ~46%.
active_experiments.rationale: You're paying for clicks on keywords like 'wireless cat water fountain' that have generated ZERO sales after $37+ in spend over 4 weeks. Every click costs $1-1.50 and none convert. Shoppers clicking these broad keywords may be looking for cheaper plastic fountains, not a $50 stainless steel one. By adding these as negative keywords, you tell Amazon to stop showing your ad for these specific searches, so your budget goes only to keywords that have a chance of converting.
active_experiments.expected_impact: Stops ~46% of wasted ad spend immediately. At the current $15/day rate, this saves $7-10/day ($50-70/week). Your remaining budget gets focused on searches more likely to convert, improving overall return on ad spend.

success_criteria.how_to_measure: Check Campaign Manager spend report after 7 days

active_experiments.element: PPC - Pause Close Automatic Campaign
active_experiments.hypothesis: Pausing the auto campaign ($15/day budget) will stop irrelevant search term matches and reduce wasted spend by ~33%.
active_experiments.rationale: The automatic campaign lets Amazon decide which searches to show your ad for. Amazon is matching your ad to irrelevant or low-intent searches that cost money but never result in a sale. With only 20 reviews vs competitors with 590+, your listing can't compete on broad searches. Pausing stops this bleeding entirely. You can restart it later when reviews and CVR improve.
active_experiments.expected_impact: Saves approximately $15/day ($105/week) by eliminating auto-targeted ad spend that has produced zero conversions. The new settlement period started at $0, so pausing now minimizes your 4/25 charge.

active_experiments.rationale: When shoppers search for cat water fountains, they scan titles in the search results before clicking. '30-Day Battery Life' answers their #1 question immediately: how often do I need to charge this? 7 out of 10 top competitors already feature battery life in their title, which means shoppers are trained to look for it. Without it, Paw Pure looks like it's hiding something. Adding it makes the listing competitive at the title level, which is the first thing shoppers see on mobile.
active_experiments.expected_impact: Higher click-through rate (CTR) from search results because shoppers can see the battery spec before clicking. Higher conversion rate (CVR) because battery life is no longer a question mark. Expected 10-20% improvement in CTR within 14 days as Amazon re-indexes the listing.

active_experiments.element: Backend search terms - add high-SV gap keywords
active_experiments.hypothesis: Adding high-SV gap keywords to backend search terms will improve organic indexing and increase sessions by 15%+ within 14 days.
active_experiments.data_trigger: 4 keyword gaps totaling 19,300 SV where Paw Pure is not indexed.
active_experiments.rationale: Right now, when someone searches 'battery operated cat fountain' (7,200 monthly searches) or 'quiet cat water fountain' (4,100 monthly searches), Paw Pure doesn't show up at all because Amazon hasn't indexed those words for your listing. Backend search terms are hidden keywords that tell Amazon what your product is. By adding these gap keywords, Amazon's algorithm will start matching your listing to those searches. This is completely invisible to shoppers, so there's zero risk of confusing anyone or hurting your current listing.
active_experiments.expected_impact: Within 24-48 hours, Amazon will re-index the listing for these new keywords. Within 14 days, you should see an increase in organic sessions (people finding your listing without ads) from searches like 'battery operated cat fountain', 'quiet cat water fountain', and 'cordless cat water fountain'. Combined search volume of the gap keywords is 19,300/month. Even capturing 1% of that = ~193 new sessions/month for free.

success_criteria.how_to_measure: Business Reports > Sessions column. Check if organic sessions increased after 48 hours.

active_experiments.element: PPC - Exact match campaign for converting targets
active_experiments.hypothesis: Creating a new exact-match campaign with proven converting targets at $10/day will produce sub-33.5% ACoS.
active_experiments.rationale: Your current campaigns use broad and automatic targeting, which means Amazon shows your ad for loosely related searches that waste money. Exact match tells Amazon to ONLY show your ad when someone types the exact keyword you choose. The 3 keywords selected ('battery operated cat fountain' at 7,200 monthly searches, 'quiet cat water fountain' at 4,100, 'cordless cat water fountain' at 8,900) directly describe what Paw Pure is. Shoppers typing these exact phrases are looking for exactly your product, so they're much more likely to buy. The 'Dynamic bids, down only' strategy means Amazon will automatically lower your bid when it thinks a click is unlikely to convert, protecting your budget.
active_experiments.expected_impact: At $10/day with exact match targeting, expect a 2-5% conversion rate on these keywords vs the current 0%. That translates to 1-3 sales per week from ads alone. Target ACoS below 33.5% (your breakeven). If this works, it proves paid ads can be profitable and gives you a template to scale with more keywords.

success_criteria.how_to_measure: Campaign Manager > Manual Exact Campaign > Sales column

active_experiments._notes: Run AFTER EXP-024/025/026 stabilize. Requires Campaign Manager access.

active_experiments.element: Listing audit - investigate ad traffic vs organic traffic CVR gap
active_experiments.hypothesis: The 5.13% organic CVR vs 0% ad CVR gap indicates mismatched ad targeting intent.
active_experiments.rationale: There's a telling gap in your data: shoppers who find Paw Pure organically (through search) convert at 5.13%, but shoppers who click your ads convert at 0%. This means the ads are attracting the wrong shoppers. The Search Terms Report will show you exactly which search phrases Amazon matched your ads to. By identifying the mismatched terms (for example, people searching for 'cheap cat fountain' or 'plastic cat fountain' seeing your $50 stainless steel product), you can either negate those terms or restructure your campaigns. This is detective work that directly informs EXP-024 and EXP-027.
active_experiments.expected_impact: No direct revenue impact since this is a diagnostic step, but the findings will make every other PPC experiment more effective. Understanding which search terms waste money lets you negate them (saving $5-15/day), and understanding which terms convert lets you bid on them specifically. This could reveal 5-10 additional negative keywords beyond what EXP-024 already identified.

active_experiments.element: Inventory - FBA removal/liquidation assessment for excess fountain stock
active_experiments.hypothesis: Removing 200+ excess fountain units via FBA removal or liquidation will reduce monthly storage fees by ~$130/month, improving contribution margin by 23%.
active_experiments.rationale: You have 377 fountain units sitting in Amazon's warehouse, enough inventory for 1,412 days (almost 4 years) at current sales velocity. Amazon charges you $246.63 every month to store them, which is more than your monthly gross profit of $120.16. In other words, storage fees alone are eating all your profit and then some. By removing 200-300 units (either shipping them home or liquidating), you cut storage fees by $130+/month immediately. Keeping 60-77 units gives you 6-9 months of cover, which is plenty of runway to grow sales.
active_experiments.expected_impact: Removing 200 units saves approximately $130/month in storage fees, improving contribution margin by 23%. The one-time cost to ship units home is about $100 (at $0.50/unit), which pays for itself in less than 1 month of storage savings. Over 6 months, that's $780 saved. If you liquidate instead, Amazon pays you 5-10% of retail (~$5-10/unit), so you recover $1,000-2,000 while also eliminating the storage drag.

success_criteria.how_to_measure: Payments > Fee Preview or Inventory > Monthly Storage Fee column

active_experiments.element: Reviews - systematic Request-a-Review campaign for all delivered orders
active_experiments.hypothesis: Sending Request-a-Review to all delivered fountain and filter orders will increase review velocity from 0.65/week to 2+/week within 30 days, improving social proof and CVR.
active_experiments.rationale: Reviews are the #1 reason shoppers pick a competitor over Paw Pure. You have 20 reviews while competitors range from 590 to 15,320. At your current rate of 0.65 reviews per week, it would take over 3 years just to reach 100 reviews. Amazon's 'Request a Review' button is a free, TOS-compliant way to ask every delivered customer to leave a review. Most sellers don't use it consistently, which means reviews trickle in randomly. By clicking this button for every delivered order twice a week, you systematically capture reviews from the 5-15% of customers who are willing to leave one. This is the single most important long-term action for closing the competitive gap.
active_experiments.expected_impact: Expected to increase review velocity from 0.65/week to 1.5-2.0/week within 30 days. At 2 reviews per week, you reach 50 reviews in ~4 months and 100 reviews in ~10 months instead of 3 years. Every 10 new reviews improves CVR by an estimated 0.5-1.0% because shoppers trust products with more social proof. The filter listing also benefits since filter customers tend to leave positive reviews (35.71% CVR suggests high satisfaction).

success_criteria.how_to_measure: Count reviews on product page weekly. Track date of each new review.

completed_experiments.hypothesis: Adding 'for Cats & Small Dogs' to title will index for dog keywords and increase sessions.
completed_experiments.rationale: 'Small dog water fountain wireless' has 3,200 monthly searches, and Paw Pure wasn't showing up for any dog-related keywords because 'dog' wasn't in the title. Adding 'for Cats & Small Dogs' to the title tells Amazon to index the listing for both cat AND small dog searches, effectively opening a second audience with zero additional cost. The fountain works perfectly for small dogs, so there's no misleading claim.
completed_experiments.expected_impact: Indexes Paw Pure for dog-related fountain keywords totaling 3,200+ monthly searches. Even capturing 1% of those searches adds ~32 new sessions per month for free. Also positions the product for the less competitive 'small dog water fountain' niche where review counts are lower and Paw Pure can compete more effectively.
